objections.objection: The timeline and sequencing for raising the tracking age (Parliament amendment in year 1; teacher-professionalization year 2–10; school conversion year 3–9) is infeasible because it ignores two binding capacity and legal constraints documented elsewhere in the record: (1) the felmenő rendszer (ascending-system) requires an 8-year, cohort-protected phase-out that fixes the minimal legislative-to-completion window and thus cannot be compressed into a 1–9 year package as written; (2) Hungary’s teacher labour-market constraints (half the workforce aged 50+, only ~7% under 30) plus the concurrent 2024–2031 salary reform materially restrict available administrative/bandwidth and retraining capacity during 2024–2031, making the proposed large-scale professionalization and reallocation programme unrealistic to deliver on the proposed schedule. The implementation steps also fail to specify any legally realistic mechanism to compel or incentivise church/foundation maintainers to convert 6/8-year tracks—despite the scenario elsewhere acknowledging their constitutionally- and contractually-protected autonomy—so the conversion steps lack a legal path to completion.
objections.suggested_revision: Insert a pre-legislative teacher-workforce feasibility and legal-mapping stage (6–12 months) that: (a) models retirements/attrition and net recruitment under the 2024–2031 salary reform and produces a published capacity report; (b) produces a legal opinion on the felmenő rendszer timing and on instruments available to influence non-state maintainers (conditional funding clauses, MOUs, negotiated conversion agreements, or required primary-law changes). Revise the timeline to align with felmenő minimums (explicitly schedule an 8-year cohort phase-out) and sequence pilot conversions regionally over 10–15 years. Add named implementation owners and resourcing: a funded national teacher-retraining plan with annual intake targets, a phased regional pilot (3–5 districts) to demonstrate conversion, and a cross-party entrenchment strategy (or cardinal-law design) to reduce reversal risk before national roll-out.

objections.objection: The Klebelsberg Centre–led quick formalisation of minimum-score thresholds/capacities in year 1–2 presumes administrative and legal authority that the Centre does not unilaterally possess over church and foundation maintainers; the steps ignore the documented strong growth and autonomy of non-state gimnázium maintainers and therefore omit the legal lead time, consultation requirements, and likely litigation or funding-reliance responses that would delay or block enforcement. The plan also omits concrete teacher-staffing and labour-relations steps: an abrupt shrinkage of state-sector selective places across ~200 schools will trigger redeployment, redundancy, and collective-bargaining obligations that require weeks/months of formal processes and budgeted compensatory funding—which the implementation steps do not budget or schedule.
objections.suggested_revision: Before Klebelsberg issues binding thresholds, commission a rapid legal-authority audit (1–3 months) and develop two parallel tracks: (A) a pilot regional cap/threshold programme in a limited set of tankerülets (3–6 regions) with pre-agreed appeals and judicial-review protocols; (B) a national incentive/regulatory package to limit compensatory expansion by non-state maintainers (legal opinion -> conditional funding or negotiated MOUs). Add a fully costed teacher-redeployment/compensation plan with explicit timelines for consultation and collective-bargaining processes, and extend the initial implementation timeline to 3–5 years with staged pilots, monitoring, and contingency funding for litigation/maintainer responses.

objections.objection: The decree-amendment + kormányhivatal monitoring timeline (decree drafting year 1; pilot year 1–2; monitoring capacity year 2–4) assumes decree-level instruments will be sufficient to bind maintainers and that the kormányhivatal can rapidly build the inspection, data-linkage, and enforcement capacity needed to ensure compliance across state, church and foundation schools. This contradicts the scenario’s own admission that church/foundation maintainers enjoy autonomy and that enforcement across ~2,000+ institutions is multi-year and likely requires new inspection capacity. The coaching-resistant exam pilot is also scheduled too short—behavioural adaptation by private tutors and families (shift to earlier tutoring, informal networks) typically unfolds over multiple admission cycles and will not be detectable in a single 1–2 year pilot.
objections.suggested_revision: Add an explicit legal-clarification step (0–6 months) to test whether an EMMI decree can lawfully bind non-state maintainers or whether primary legislation or conditional-funding instruments are required. Budget and schedule a Kormányhivatal capacity-build (staffing, IT/data linkage, sanctioning authority) during year 1–3 with clear FTE and IT milestones, and attach enforcement triggers (graduated sanctions, conditional grants) to compliance. Extend the coaching-resistant exam pilot to cover at least 3–4 admission cycles (3–5 years) with pre-specified evaluation metrics (prep-spending surveys, applicant SES composition, matched-cohort outcomes) and an adaptive design that permits iterative item-bank reform and monitoring of private-prep displacement channels.
